There are roughly 22,000 operational [desalination](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination "Desalination") plants, located across 177 countries, which generate an estimated 95 million m3/day of fresh water to over 300 million people daily.[\[1\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-1)[\[2\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-2) Micro desalination plants operate near almost every natural gas or [fracking](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fracking "Fracking") facility in the United States. Furthermore, micro desalination facilities exist in textile, leather, food industries, etc.[\[3\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-3)

As of December 7, 2023, [Shoaiba Power and Desalination Plant](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Shoaiba_Power_and_Desalination_Plant "Shoaiba Power and Desalination Plant"), located on the Red Sea coast, managed by the Saline Water Conversion Corporation, holds the record for the highest production capacity globally, generating 2,998,000 cubic meters of desalinated water per day.[\[4\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-4)

There are multiple types of water desalination systems but the two most used are thermal and membrane. Thermal systems use heat to evaporate the water, separating it from the salt and then condensing the steam to collect the fresh water. Membrane systems use high pressure to push the water through a series of sieves. The sieves membranes are big enough to allow the water to pass through but small enough to trap the salt, minerals and other impurities.[\[5\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-5)

## Operating desalination plants

The following table is a list of operating desalination plants. Criteria for inclusion on this list include:

- that it's operating (not proposed or decommissioned), and
- has a capacity of at least 100,000 cubic meters per day or it's the largest in its country.
- excludes desalination plants used primarily for mining

## Algeria

\[ [edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Desalination_by_country&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Algeria")\]

[Algeria](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Algeria "Algeria") ranks 2nd in the [Mediterranean](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mediterranean_Sea "Mediterranean Sea") with a desalinated water production capacity of (631 million m3/year), followed by [Spain](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain "Spain") (405 million m3/year) and [Egypt](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Egypt "Egypt") (200 million m3/year).[\[20\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-20) is believed to have at least 20 desalination plants in operation.

- Arzew IWPP Power & Desalination Plant, Arzew, 90,000m3/day[\[21\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-UNESCWA-21)
- Cap Djinet Seawater Reverse Osmosis 100,000 m3/day[\[22\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-22)
- Tlemcen Souk Tleta 200,000 m3/day
- Tlemcen Hounaine 200,000 m3/day
- Beni Saf 200,000 m3/day
- Tenes 200,000 m3/day
- Fouka 120,000 m3/day
- Tipaza 100,000 m3/day[\[21\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-UNESCWA-21)
- Skikda 100,000 m3/day
- Hamma Seawater Desalination Plant 200,000 m3/day built by General Electric[\[23\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-23)
- Mostaganem, (Sonaghter) 200,000 m3/day[\[24\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-24)
- Magtaa Reverse Osmosis (RO) Desalination Plant 500,000 m3/day, Oran

## Aruba

\[ [edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Desalination_by_country&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Aruba")\]

The island of [Aruba](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Aruba "Aruba") has a large (world's largest at the time of its inauguration)\[ _[citation needed](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")_\] desalination plant, with a total installed capacity of 11.1 million US gallons (42,000 m3) per day.\[ _[citation needed](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")_\][\[25\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-25)

## Australia

\[ [edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Desalination_by_country&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Australia")\]

Main article: [Seawater desalination in Australia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Seawater_desalination_in_Australia "Seawater desalination in Australia")

The [Millennium Drought](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2000s_Australian_drought "2000s Australian drought") (1997–2009) led to a water supply crisis across much of the country. A combination of increased water usage and lower rainfall/drought in Australia caused state governments to turn to [desalination](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_desalination_plants_in_Australia "List of desalination plants in Australia"). As a result, several large-scale desalination plants were constructed ( [see list](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_desalination_plants_in_Australia "List of desalination plants in Australia")).

Large-scale seawater reverse osmosis plants (SWRO) now contribute to the domestic water supplies of several major Australian cities including Adelaide, Melbourne, Sydney, Perth and the Gold Coast. While desalination helped secure water supplies, it is energy intensive (≈$140/ML). In 2010, a [Seawater Greenhouse](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Seawater_Greenhouse "Seawater Greenhouse") went into operation in Port Augusta.[\[26\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-26)[\[27\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-27)[\[28\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-28)

A growing number of smaller scale SWRO plants are used by the oil and gas industry (both on and offshore), by mining companies to supply slurry pipelines for the transport of ore and on offshore islands to supply tourists and residents.

## Bahrain

\[ [edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Desalination_by_country&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Bahrain")\]

Completed in 2000, the Al Hidd Desalination Plant on [Muharraq](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Muharraq "Muharraq") island employed a multistage flash process, and produces 272,760 m3 (9,632,000 cu ft) per day.[\[29\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-29) The Al Hidd distillate forwarding station provides 410 million liters of distillate water storage in a series of 45-million-liter steel tanks. A 135-million-liters/day forwarding pumping station sends flows to the Hidd, Muharraq, Hoora, Sanabis, and Seef blending stations, and which has an option for gravity supply for low flows to blending pumps and pumps which forward to Janusan, Budiya and Saar.[\[30\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-30)

Upon completion of the third construction phase, the [Durrat Al Bahrain](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Durrat_Al_Bahrain "Durrat Al Bahrain") seawater reverse osmosis (SWRO) desalination plant was planned to have a capacity of 36,000 cubic meters of potable water per day to serve the irrigation needs of the Durrat Al Bahrain development.[\[31\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-31) The Bahrain-based utility company, Energy Central Co contracted to design, build and operate the plant.[\[32\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-32)

## Barbados

\[ [edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Desalination_by_country&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Barbados")\]

In 1994–1995 the island of Barbados experienced a severe 1 in 50 year severe drought[\[33\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-33) that knocked much of the island's drinking water supply offline including the country's sole major hospital in the capital-city Bridgetown.[\[34\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-34) An agreement was negotiated with [General Electric](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/General_Electric "General Electric")'s [Ionics Inc.](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Ionics_Inc.&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ionics Inc. (page does not exist)")[\[35\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-35) to build a reverse osmosis desalination plant on the south western coast of the island capable of supplying 20% of the islands population. The plant began operating within 15 months and was officially commissioned February 2000. Currently many cruise ships purchase water from Barbados due to its good quality.[\[36\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-36)

## Chile

\[ [edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Desalination_by_country&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Chile")\]

See also: [Chilean water crisis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chilean_water_crisis "Chilean water crisis") and [copper mining in Chile](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Copper_mining_in_Chile "Copper mining in Chile")

Desalination plants fully supply the Chilean cities of [Antofagasta](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Antofagasta "Antofagasta"), [Caldera](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Caldera,_Chile "Caldera, Chile") and [Mejillones](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mejillones "Mejillones").[\[40\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-rifourg-40) [Mining in Chile](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mining_in_Chile "Mining in Chile") consumes most desalinated water and this demand is driven by both the a need for more water to process [copper ores](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Copper_ore "Copper ore") with decreasing [grades](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ore_grade "Ore grade") and restrictions on the use of freshwater.[\[41\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-chilcoweb24-41) As of 2023, 9% of the water demand in Chilean mines was supplied by sea water of which 69% was desalinated.[\[41\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-chilcoweb24-41)[\[42\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-42) Twenty of Chile's 24 desalination plants (as of 2025) provide water for the [mining industry](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mining_in_Chile "Mining in Chile").[\[40\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-rifourg-40)

- Copiapó Desalination Plant[\[43\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-43)
- Thorium Power Canada, with its affiliate, DBI Chile, have proposed plans to build a 10 MW demonstration [thorium](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Thorium "Thorium") reactor in Chile to power the 20 million litre/day desalination plant. All land and regulatory approvals are currently in process.[\[44\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-44)\[ _[needs update](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")_\]

## China

\[ [edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Desalination_by_country&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: China")\]

China operates the Beijing Desalination Plant in [Tianjin](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tianjin "Tianjin"), a combination desalination and coal-fired power plant designed to alleviate Tianjin's critical water shortage. Though the facility has the capacity to produce 200,000 cubic metres (7,100,000 cu ft) of potable water per day, it has never operated at more than one-quarter capacity due to difficulties with local
companies and inadequate local infrastructure.[\[45\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-45)

The Hong Kong [Water Supplies Department](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Water_Supplies_Department "Water Supplies Department") had pilot desalination plants in [Tuen Mun](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tuen_Mun "Tuen Mun") and [Ap Lei Chau](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ap_Lei_Chau "Ap Lei Chau") using reverse-osmosis technology. The production cost was put at HK$7.8 to HK$8.4 /m3.[\[46\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-autogenerated2007-46)[\[47\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-autogenerated1-47) Hong Kong used to have a desalination plant in [Lok On Pai](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Lok_On_Pai&action=edit&redlink=1 "Lok On Pai (page does not exist)"), [Siu Lam](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Siu_Lam "Siu Lam").[\[48\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-autogenerated2003-48)

In 2014, the government confirmed the reservation of a 10-hectare site at [Tseung Kwan O](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tseung_Kwan_O "Tseung Kwan O") for the construction of a reverse-osmosis desalination plant with an initial output capacity of 50 million cubic metres per annum. Plans include provisions for future expansion to an ultimate capacity of 90 million cubic metres per annum, which will meet about 10 per cent of Hong Kong's fresh water demand. Detailed feasibility studies, preliminary design and a cost-effectiveness analysis are planned to be completed by 2014. A commissioning date of 2020 is envisaged.[\[49\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-autogenerated3-49)

## Germany

\[ [edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Desalination_by_country&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Germany")\]

Fresh water on the island of [Helgoland](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Helgoland "Helgoland") is supplied by two [reverse osmosis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Reverse_osmosis "Reverse osmosis") desalination plants.[\[54\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-54)

## Gibraltar

\[ [edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Desalination_by_country&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Gibraltar")\]

Fresh water in [Gibraltar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gibraltar "Gibraltar") is supplied by a number of [reverse osmosis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Reverse_osmosis "Reverse osmosis") and [multistage flash](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Multi-stage_flash_distillation "Multi-stage flash distillation") desalination plants.[\[55\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-55) A demonstration [forward osmosis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Forward_osmosis "Forward osmosis") desalination plant also operates there.[\[56\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-56)

## India

\[ [edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Desalination_by_country&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: India")\]

India has two large desalinization plants for domestic uses, the [Minjur Seawater Desalination Plant](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Minjur_Seawater_Desalination_Plant "Minjur Seawater Desalination Plant")[\[57\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-57)[\[58\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-58) and the [Nemmeli plant](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nemmeli_Seawater_Desalination_Plant "Nemmeli Seawater Desalination Plant"), both in [Chennai](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chennai "Chennai") with 100 million litres per day (MLPD) capacity.[\[59\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-59)[\[60\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-60) For industrial uses, 100 MLPD plant was set up in [Dahej](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dahej "Dahej"), Gujarat in 2022 with an investment cost of approximately ₹900 crore (US$94 million).[\[61\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-61)

An assumption is that around 400,000 m3/d of historic and newly installed capacity is operational in Iran.[\[62\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-GWI-62) In terms of technology, Iran's existing desalination plants use a mix of thermal processes and RO. MSF is the most widely used thermal technology although MED and vapour compression (VC) also feature.[\[62\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-GWI-62)

Iranian project Persian Gulf Water Transfer WASCO will see the biggest desalination project in the world based in Bandar Abas with 1.6 million capacity. There are three phases, phase 1 will be fully complete by 2024.[\[63\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-63)[\[64\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-64)

## Israel

\[ [edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Desalination_by_country&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Israel")\]

Israel Desalination Enterprises' Sorek Desalination Plant north of [Palmachim](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Palmachim "Palmachim") was foreseen to provide up to 26,000 m3 of potable water per hour once it went online in June 2013 (that is ca. 228 million m3 when projected on an entire year).[\[65\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-65) Once unthinkable, given Israel's history of drought and lack of available fresh water resources, with desalination Israel can now produce a surplus of fresh water.[\[66\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-66)

By 2014, Israel's desalination programs provided roughly 35% of Israel's drinking water, about 50% in 2015, and it is expected to supply 70% by 2050.[\[67\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-67) As of May 29, 2015 more than 50 percent of the water for Israeli households, agriculture and industry is artificially produced.[\[68\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-Kershner-68)

## Kazakhstan

\[ [edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Desalination_by_country&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Kazakhstan")\]

MAEK-Kazatomprom LLP operates sea water desalination plant in Aktau, Mangystau from 1967. Now its power comes to 120,000 m3/day after modernizing in 2023. Earlier it was a part of combined combinate with Nuclear plant and gas electric power stations. Aktau also has "Kaspiy"- membrane technology water desalination plant, which power comes to 20,000 m3/day. Kendirli desalination plant was inaugurated in July 2025 south of the city of Zhanaozen, and it has capacity to produce 50,000 m3/day.

There are concerns about the ability to produce water since Caspian sea levels are dropping.[\[80\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-80)

## Kuwait

\[ [edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Desalination_by_country&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Kuwait")\]

[Kuwait](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kuwait#Water_and_sanitation "Kuwait") does not have any permanent rivers. It does have some [wadis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wadis "Wadis"), the most notable of which is Wadi Al-Batin which forms the border between Kuwait and Iraq.

Kuwait relies on water desalination as a primary source of fresh water for drinking and domestic purposes. There are currently more than six desalination plants.[\[81\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-fao-81) Kuwait was the first country in the world to use desalination to supply water for large-scale domestic use. The history of desalination in Kuwait dates back to 1951 when the first distillation plant was commissioned.[\[82\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-scidi-82)

## Norway

\[ [edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Desalination_by_country&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Norway")\]

[Norway](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Norway "Norway") is a country with little to no problems with water access. Over 99% of the population's water supply comes from fresh water sources such as lakes, tarns, rivers and ground water. There are however three water works in Norway taking use of desalination of sea water and all of them are located in the county of [Nordland](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nordland "Nordland"), only providing around 500 people with water.[\[89\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-89)

## Palestine

\[ [edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Desalination_by_country&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Palestine")\]

This section is an excerpt from [Water supply and sanitation in Palestine § Desalinated seawater](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Water_supply_and_sanitation_in_Palestine#Desalinated_seawater "Water supply and sanitation in Palestine").\[ [edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Water_supply_and_sanitation_in_Palestine&action=edit)\]

As of 2007, there was one seawater desalination plant in [Deir al-Balah](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Deir_al-Balah "Deir al-Balah") in the Gaza Strip, built in 1997–99 with funding by the Austrian government. It has a capacity of 600 cubic metres (21,000 cu ft) per day and it is owned and operated by the Coastal Municipalities Water Utility. At least initially, the operating costs were subsidized by the Austrian government. The desalinated water is distributed to 13 [water kiosks](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Water_kiosk "Water kiosk").[\[94\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-94)[\[95\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-95)

For over 20 years, a major desalination plant for Gaza has been discussed. The Palestinian Water Authority has approved a $500 million facility. The New York Times reported in 2013 that Israel supported this and had begun to offer Palestinians desalination training.[\[96\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-96) In 2012 the French government committed a 10 million-euro grant for the plant. Arab countries, coordinated by the [Islamic Development Bank](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Islamic_Development_Bank "Islamic Development Bank"), committed to provide half of the necessary funds, matching an expected European financial commitment. The [European Investment Bank](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/European_Investment_Bank "European Investment Bank") provides technical assistance.[\[97\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-97)

Another major problem is that desalination is very energy-intensive, while the import of fuel to produce the necessary electricity is restricted by Israel and Egypt. Furthermore, revenues from drinking water tariffs are insufficient to cover the operating costs of the envisaged plant at the current tariff level.[\[98\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-98)

## Spain

[Lanzarote](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lanzarote "Lanzarote") is the easternmost of the autonomous [Canary Islands](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Canary_Islands "Canary Islands"), which are of volcanic origin. It is the closest of the islands to the [Sahara](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sahara "Sahara") desert and therefore the driest, and it has limited water supplies. A private, commercial desalination plant was installed in 1964 to serve the whole island and enable the tourism industry. In 1974, the venture was injected with investments from local and municipal governments, and a larger infrastructure was put in place in 1989, the Lanzarote Island Waters Consortium (INALSA)[\[111\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-111) was formed.

A prototype [seawater greenhouse](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Seawater_greenhouse "Seawater greenhouse") was constructed in Tenerife in 1992.[\[112\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-112)

## South Africa

\[ [edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Desalination_by_country&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: South Africa")\]

Numerous towns in South Africa have operational reverse osmosis plants, with the first - the Albany Coast RO plant - having been installed in 1997. Most existing plants are used in areas with scarce access to freshwater supplies, or for energy production projects. However, these plants are small, and no major city has as yet built a large-scale desalination facility.[\[113\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-Desalination_An_Alternative_Solution_To_Sa%E2%80%99s_Water_Supply_Challenges-113)

SA's in-operation desalination plants are below.

- [Witsand Solar Desalination Plant](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Witsand_Solar_Desalination_Plant "Witsand Solar Desalination Plant"): 300 m3/day
- Mossel Bay: 15,000 m3/day[\[114\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-114)
- Transnet Saldanha: 2,400 m3/day[\[115\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-115)
- Knysna: 2,000 m3/day[\[116\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-116)
- Plettenberg Bay: 2,000 m3/day[\[117\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-117)
- Bushman's River Mouth: 1,800 m3/day[\[118\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-118)
- Lambert's Bay: 1,700 m3/day[\[119\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-119)
- Cannon Rocks: 750 m3/day[\[120\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-120)

In September 2024, the [City of Cape Town](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/City_of_Cape_Town "City of Cape Town") announced plans to build a major desalination plant to supplement 11% of the [City of Cape Town](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/City_of_Cape_Town "City of Cape Town")'s water supply by 2030. [Cape Town](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cape_Town "Cape Town") is South Africa's second-largest city by population (at around 5 million residents as of 2025).[\[121\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-Cape_Town_to_wrap_up_desalination_plant_feasibility_study_in_2025,_first_water_possible_in_2030-121)[\[122\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-City_progresses_with_plans_for_permanent_desalination_plant-122)

As part of the city's _New Water Program_, the desalination plant will be situated in the [light industrial](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Light_industrial "Light industrial") suburb of [Paarden Eiland](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Paarden_Eiland "Paarden Eiland"), and will produce between 50 million and 70 million liters of water per day. The plant will form part of the Program's broader efforts to bolster water security across the [metro](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Metropolitan_municipality_(South_Africa) "Metropolitan municipality (South Africa)"). The _New Water Program_ aims to add 300 million liters of water per day to Cape Town's supply, via a 10-year, R120 billion infrastructure investment.[\[123\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-Paarden_Eiland_Desalination_Plant-123)

## Sweden

\[ [edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Desalination_by_country&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Sweden")\]

While mainland Sweden is able to depend on long rivers, many thousands of lakes and groundwater, the sunny and dry nature of the Baltic Sea archipelago has led to a deficit of water on the island of [Gotland](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gotland "Gotland").[\[124\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-124) The island has two desalination plants for brackish water from the [Baltic Sea](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Baltic_Sea "Baltic Sea"), one built 2016 in Herrvik with a capacity to produce 480 m3/day,[\[125\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-125) and another one with a capacity of 7 500 m3/day in Kvarnåkershamn.[\[126\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-126)

In February 2021 a desalination plant with a daily capacity of 13000 tons was built as an answer to a water emergency. The plant supposed to support semiconductor production in greater Hscinchu area from Nanliao.[\[127\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-taiwan-127)

## Trinidad and Tobago

\[ [edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Desalination_by_country&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Trinidad and Tobago")\]

The [Republic of Trinidad and Tobago](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Republic_of_Trinidad_and_Tobago "Republic of Trinidad and Tobago") uses desalination to open up more of the island's water supply for drinking purposes. The country's desalination plant, opened in March 2003, is considered to be the first of its kind. It was the largest desalination facility in the Americas, and it processes 28,800,000 US gallons (109,000 m3) of water a day at the price of $2.67 per 1,000 US gallons (3.8 m3).[\[128\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-trinidad-128)

This plant will be located at Trinidad's [Point Lisas](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Point_Lisas "Point Lisas") Industrial Estate, a park of more than 12 companies in various manufacturing and processing functions, and it will allow for easy access to water for both factories and residents in the country.[\[129\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-tobago-129)

## United Arab Emirates

\[ [edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Desalination_by_country&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: United Arab Emirates")\]

The UAE relies has over 70 desalination plants, and relies on desalination for 42% of its drinking water.[\[130\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-130) The hosts some of the world's largest desalination plants, which are listed in the table above.[\[131\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-131) The include the Jebel Ali desalination plant in Dubai, a dual-purpose facility, uses [multistage flash distillation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Multistage_flash_distillation "Multistage flash distillation") and is the largest in the world, capable of producing 2,227,600 m3 (2,913,600 cu yd) of water per day. Al Taweelah RO is the world's largest reverse osmosis desalination plant, producing 909,200 m3 (1,189,200 cu yd) of water per day.[\[132\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-132)

Some of the smaller ones include:

- Kalba 15,000 m3/day built for Sharjah Electricity and Water Authority completed 2010 (operator CH2MHill)[\[133\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-133)
- Khor Fakkan 22,500 m3/day (operator CH2MHill)
- Ghalilah RAK 68,000 m3/day (operator Aquatech)
- Hamriyah 90,000 m3/day (operator Aqua Engineering)[\[106\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-Aquatech-106)
- Al Zawrah 27,000 m3/day (operator Aqua Engineering)
- Layyah I 22,500 m3/day (operator CH2MHill)
- Emayil & Saydiat Island ≈20,000 m3/day (operator Aqua EPC)
- Al Yasat Al Soghrih Island 2M gallons per day (GPD) or 9,000 m3/day
- A seawater greenhouse was constructed on Al Aryam Island, Abu Dhabi, United Arab Emirates in 2000.

## United Kingdom and Crown dependencies

\[ [edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Desalination_by_country&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: United Kingdom and Crown dependencies")\]

The first large-scale plant in the [United Kingdom](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"), the [Thames Water Desalination Plant](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Thames_Water_Desalination_Plant "Thames Water Desalination Plant"), was built in [Beckton](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Beckton "Beckton"), east London for [Thames Water](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Thames_Water "Thames Water") by [Acciona](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Acciona "Acciona") Agua.[\[134\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-134) It was built in 2010 at a cost of £250m. The plant provides up to 150 million litres of drinking water each day (150,000 cubic metres) – enough for nearly one million people.

### Jersey

\[ [edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Desalination_by_country&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Jersey")\]

The desalination plant located near La Rosière, [La Corbière](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/La_Corbi%C3%A8re "La Corbière"), Jersey, is operated by [Jersey Water](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Jersey_Water&action=edit&redlink=1 "Jersey Water (page does not exist)"). Built in 1970 in an abandoned quarry, it was the first in the British Isles.

The original plant used a [multistage flash (MSF)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Multistage_flash_distillation "Multistage flash distillation") distillation process, whereby seawater was boiled under vacuum, evaporated and condensed into a freshwater distillate. In 1997, the MSF plant reached the end of its operational life and was replaced with a modern reverse osmosis plant.

Its maximum power demand is 1,750 kW, and the output capacity is 6,000 cubic meters per day. Specific energy consumption is 6.8 kWh/m3.[\[135\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-135)

## United States

\[ [edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Desalination_by_country&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: United States")\]

### Texas

\[ [edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Desalination_by_country&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Texas")\]

There are a dozen different desalination projects in the [state of Texas](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/State_of_Texas "State of Texas"), both for desalinating groundwater and desalinating [seawater](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Seawater "Seawater") from the [Gulf of Mexico](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gulf_of_Mexico "Gulf of Mexico"). Corpus Christi is currently in the process of finding a contractor to start the development of one in the Corpus Christi ship channel.[\[136\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-136) However, currently there are no seawater desalination plants earmarked for municipal purposes.[\[137\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-137)[\[138\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-138)

- El Paso: [Brackish](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Brackish_water "Brackish water") groundwater has been treated at the [El Paso, Texas](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/El_Paso,_Texas "El Paso, Texas"), plant since around 2004. It produces 27,500,000 US gallons (104,000,000 L; 22,900,000 imp gal) of fresh water daily (about 25% of total freshwater deliveries) by reverse osmosis.[\[139\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-139) The plant's water cost – largely representing the cost of energy – is about 2.1 times higher than ordinary groundwater production.

### California

\[ [edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Desalination_by_country&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: California")\]

California has 17 desalination plants in the works, either partially constructed or through exploration and planning phases.[\[140\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-pricey-140) The list of locations includes [Bay Point](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bay_Point,_California "Bay Point, California"), in the Delta, [Redwood City](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Redwood_City,_California "Redwood City, California"), seven in the [Santa Cruz](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Santa_Cruz,_California "Santa Cruz, California") / [Monterey Bay](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Monterey_Bay "Monterey Bay"), [Cambria](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cambria,_California "Cambria, California"), Oceaneo, [Redondo Beach](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Redondo_Beach,_California "Redondo Beach, California"), [Huntington Beach](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Huntington_Beach_Desalination_Plant "Huntington Beach Desalination Plant"), [Dana Point](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dana_Point "Dana Point"), [Camp Pendleton](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Camp_Pendleton "Camp Pendleton"), [Oceanside](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oceanside,_California "Oceanside, California") and [Carlsbad](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Carlsbad,_California "Carlsbad, California").[\[141\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-141)

- Carlsbad: The [Claude "Bud" Lewis Carlsbad Desalination Plant](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Claude_%22Bud%22_Lewis_Carlsbad_Desalination_Plant "Claude \"Bud\" Lewis Carlsbad Desalination Plant") was constructed at a cost of $1 billion by Poseidon Resources and was the largest desalination plant in the United States when it went online December 14, 2015.[\[142\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-142) It produces 50 million gallons a day to 110,000 customers throughout San Diego County.
- Monterey County: [Sand City](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sand_City,_California "Sand City, California"), two miles north of Monterey, with a population of 334, is the only city in California completely supplied with water from a desalination plant.
- Santa Barbara: The Charles Meyer Desalination Facility was constructed in [Santa Barbara, California](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Santa_Barbara,_California "Santa Barbara, California"), in 1991–92 as a temporary emergency water supply in response to severe drought.[\[143\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-143)[\[144\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-144) While it has a high operating cost, the facility only needs to operate infrequently, allowing Santa Barbara to use its other supplies more extensively.[\[145\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-145)[\[146\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-146) The plant was re-activated in the spring of 2017.

### Florida

\[ [edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Desalination_by_country&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Florida")\]

[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b8/Northcapecoral-RO.jpg/250px-Northcapecoral-RO.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Northcapecoral-RO.jpg) RO production train, North Cape Coral RO Plant

In 1977, [Cape Coral](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cape_Coral "Cape Coral"), Florida became the first municipality in the United States to use the RO process on a large scale with an initial operating capacity of 3 million gallons per day. By 1985, due to the rapid growth in population of Cape Coral, the city had the largest low pressure reverse osmosis plant in the world, capable of producing 15 MGD.[\[147\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-147)

As of 2012, South Florida has 33 brackish and two seawater desalination plants operating with seven brackish water plants under construction. The brackish and seawater desalination plants have the capacity to produce 245 million gallons of potable water per day.[\[148\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-148)

The [Tampa Bay Water](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tampa_Bay_Water "Tampa Bay Water") desalination project near [Tampa, Florida](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tampa,_Florida "Tampa, Florida"), was originally a private venture led by Poseidon Resources, but it was delayed by the bankruptcy of Poseidon Resources' successive partners in the venture, Stone & Webster, then Covanta (formerly Ogden) and its principal subcontractor, Hydroanautics. Stone & Webster declared bankruptcy June 2000. Covanta and Hydranautics joined in 2001, but Covanta failed to complete the construction bonding, and then the Tampa Bay Water agency purchased the project on May 15, 2002, underwriting the project. Tampa Bay Water then contracted with Covanta Tampa Construction, which produced a project that failed performance tests. After its parent went bankrupt, Covanta also filed for bankruptcy prior to performing renovations that would have satisfied contractual agreements. This resulted in nearly six months of litigation. In 2004, Tampa Bay Water hired a renovation team, American Water/Acciona Aqua, to bring the plant to its original, anticipated design. The plant was deemed fully operational in 2007,[\[149\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-tampabaywater.org-149) and is designed to run at a maximum capacity of 25 million US gallons (95,000 m3) per day.[\[150\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-150) The plant can now produce up to 25 million US gallons (95,000 m3) per day when needed.[\[151\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-151)

### Arizona

\[ [edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Desalination_by_country&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: Arizona")\]

- [Yuma](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Yuma_Desalting_Plant "Yuma Desalting Plant"): The desalination plant in [Yuma, Arizona](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Yuma,_Arizona "Yuma, Arizona"), was constructed under authority of the state [Colorado River Basin Salinity Control Act](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Colorado_River_Basin_Salinity_Control_Act "Colorado River Basin Salinity Control Act") of 1974 to treat saline agricultural return flows from the Wellton-Mohawk Irrigation and Drainage District into the [Colorado River](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Colorado_River "Colorado River"). The treated water is intended for inclusion in water deliveries to [Mexico](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mexico "Mexico"), thereby keeping a like amount of [freshwater](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Freshwater "Freshwater") in [Lake Mead](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lake_Mead "Lake Mead"), [Arizona](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Arizona "Arizona") and [Nevada](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nevada "Nevada"). Construction of the plant was completed in 1992, and it has operated on two occasions since then. With a full capacity of 73 million gallons per day of permeate water, it is the largest desalination plant in the US. The plant has been maintained, but largely not operated due to sufficient freshwater supplies from the upper Colorado River.[\[152\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-152) An agreement was reached in April 2010 between the [Southern Nevada Water Authority](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Southern_Nevada_Water_Authority "Southern Nevada Water Authority"), the [Metropolitan Water District of Southern California](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Metropolitan_Water_District_of_Southern_California "Metropolitan Water District of Southern California"), the [Central Arizona Project](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Central_Arizona_Project "Central Arizona Project"), and the [U.S. Bureau of Reclamation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/U.S._Bureau_of_Reclamation "U.S. Bureau of Reclamation") to underwrite the cost of running the plant in a year-long pilot project.[\[153\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-153)

## Western Sahara

\[ [edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Desalination_by_country&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: Western Sahara")\]

A water desalination project is planned in [Western Sahara](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Western_Sahara "Western Sahara"), within the [area under Moroccan occupation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Moroccan-occupied_Western_Sahara "Moroccan-occupied Western Sahara"). The [Moroccan wall](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Moroccan_wall "Moroccan wall") entirely blocks the [Sahrawi Arab Democratic Republic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sahrawi_Arab_Democratic_Republic "Sahrawi Arab Democratic Republic")'s access to the sea, preventing it from establishing any desalination facilities.

| [Dakhla](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dakhla-Oued_Ed-Dahab "Dakhla-Oued Ed-Dahab") | 2018 | 30 | [\[154\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desalination_by_country#cite_note-154) |

# Does size matter? Meet ten of the world's largest desalination plants

**The recently awarded Rabigh 3 desalination project in the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia to ACWA Power was branded as one of the world’s largest [desalination plants.](https://www.aquatechtrade.com/water-stories/desalination/desalination-in-hong-kong)**

**To put it into perspective, with a capacity of 600,000 m3/day, that’s 600 million litres of water that will eventually be produced per day.**

**There is no doubting at that size, the Saudi mega-size project will be one of the largest out there and the second largest awarded RO desalination plant.**

**Yet most desalination professionals will know it’s not the largest and it raises the question of well, with over 20,000 desalination plants contracted around the world, which are the largest?**

**We set out to put together this top ten list.**

Monday, 19 April 2021

![Does size matter? Meet six of the world's largest desalination plants](https://edge.sitecorecloud.io/raiamsterda13f7-raidigitalpdb6c-productionf3f5-ef30/media/project/rai-amsterdam-xmc/aquatech/aquatech/news/2019/5/102/cover-image-6-desal-plants_1140x400.png?h=400&iar=0&w=1140)

The recently awarded Rabigh 3 desalination project in the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia to ACWA Power was branded as one of the world’s largest desalination plants.

To put it into perspective, with a capacity of 600,000 m3/day, that’s 600 million litres of water that will eventually be produced per day.

There is no doubting at that size, the Saudi mega-size project will be one of the largest out there and the second largest awarded RO desalination plant.

Yet most [desalination](https://www.aquatechtrade.com/water-stories/desalination/desalination-essential-guide) professionals will know it’s not _the_ largest and it raises the question of well, with over 20,000 desalination plants contracted around the world, which are the largest?

We set out to put together this top ten list.

## The largest desalination plants in the world

01. ### Ras Al Khair, Saudi Arabia: 1,036,000 m3/day


    ![Ras Al Khair desalination plants](https://edge.sitecorecloud.io/raiamsterda13f7-raidigitalpdb6c-productionf3f5-ef30/media/project/rai-amsterdam-xmc/aquatech/aquatech/news/2019/5/101/aqd_ras-al-khair_720x400.png)

    Commonly regarded as the desalination heavyweight of the world, the massive Ras Al-Khair is a hybrid project that uses both thermal multistage flash (MSF) and reverse osmosis (RO) technologies.

    Located 75km north-west of Jubail and serving Riyadh, the site also has a substantial power generation component, with a capacity of 2,400MW.

    The main contractor for plant construction was Doosan and its consortium partner Saudi Archirodon, with Poyry acting as the consultant for the project.

    Ras Al-Khair started operations in 2014 but in 2017 was put up for sale by the Saline Water Conversion Corporation (SWCC) to kickstart privatisation plans and the sale of its assets.

02. ### Taweelah, UAE – 909,200 m3/day


    ![Taweelah desalination plants](https://edge.sitecorecloud.io/raiamsterda13f7-raidigitalpdb6c-productionf3f5-ef30/media/project/rai-amsterdam-xmc/aquatech/aquatech/news/2019/5/101/aqd_taweelah_720x400.png)

    Despite the project being in its infancy, when complete Taweelah will catapult the UAE into the top three list.

    To date, seven consortia are bidding on the mega project, including: ACWA Power; Suez International Power SA Dubai Branch (Engie), with Marubeni Corp; Sumitomo Corp, with Veolia Middle East; Valoriza Agua, with Utico FZC and IDB Infrastructure Fund II; Cobra Instalaciones y Servicios, with Orascom Construction; Acciona Agua, with Pal Group; and Suez International, with Sojitz Corporation and NV Besix SA.

    ACWA Power with EPC consortium comprising Sepco3 and Abengoa has so far placed the lowest bid using the higher electricity tariff at AED8.26 ($2.25) at $0.49 per cubic metre.

    More than 40 companies were reported to have initially shown interest in the Taweelah tender.

    Once complete, the Taweela power and water complete is expected to raise the emirate’s proportion of desalinated produced water by RO from 13 percent today to 30 percent by 2022.

03. ### Shuaiba 3, Saudi Arabia – 880,000 m3/day


    ![Shuaiba desalination plants](https://edge.sitecorecloud.io/raiamsterda13f7-raidigitalpdb6c-productionf3f5-ef30/media/project/rai-amsterdam-xmc/aquatech/aquatech/news/2019/5/101/aqd_shuaiba_720x400.png)

    A second in the list for Saudi Arabia, the Shuaiba 3 development is located 90 kilometres south of the historic city of Jeddah.

    A consortium involving Siemens of Germany for the power plant and Doosan for the thermal desalination plant were selected by ACWA Power to provide project engineering, procurement and construction of the plant.

    One expansion to the plant has been completed and one expansion is in the final construction stage with a total additional 400,000 m3/day of RO capacity added, according to ACWA Power.

    When complete in the first half of 2019, Shuaiba will eventually overtake Ras Al Khair as the largest [operating desalination plant](https://www.aquatechtrade.com/news/desalination/desalination-essential-guide/) with total capacity of 1,282,000 m3/day.

    The development is part of wider plans from the Water and Electricity Company (WEC) to significantly increase desalination capacity in the country.

04. ### Jubail Water and Power Company (JWAP), Saudi Arabia - 800,000 m3 /day


    ![Jubail Water and Power Company](https://edge.sitecorecloud.io/raiamsterda13f7-raidigitalpdb6c-productionf3f5-ef30/media/project/rai-amsterdam-xmc/aquatech/aquatech/news/2019/4/4-jubail-water-and-power-company.jpg)

    One of the world’s most notable integrated water and power facilities (IWPP), the Jubail Plant is a joint venture between Marafiq, Saudi Electricity Company (SEC), the Water and Electricity Holding Company, and the SGA Marafiq Consortium comprising ENGIE, Gulf Investment Corporation, and ACWA Power Projects.

    Commercial operation started at the end of 2010 with a 20-year period slated term for operation. A dual-purpose facility, JWAP includes combined-cycle based power and multi-effect distillation (MED) water generation.

    The plant is split into four operational blocks. Three of the four blocks are power and desalination blocks, each of which comprises three gas turbines operating in a combined cycle with a single back pressure steam turbine feeding each nine desalination MED units. The fourth block is a ‘power only’ block with three gas turbines a one reheat condensing steam turbine.

    The plant is owned by Jubail Water and Power Company (JWAP). The off-taker is Marafiq Water and Supply Company (Tawreed), a wholly-owned subsidiary of Marafiq, which purchases all of the plant output through a 20-year Power and Water Purchase Agreement (PWPA) with JWAP.

05. ### Umm Al Quwain (UAQ), UAE – 682,900 m3/day


    ![Umm Al Quwain_new](https://edge.sitecorecloud.io/raiamsterda13f7-raidigitalpdb6c-productionf3f5-ef30/media/project/rai-amsterdam-xmc/aquatech/aquatech/news/2019/4/umm-al-quwain_new.png)
    _Image credit: Sidem Veolia_
    Based in the Emirate of Umm Al Quwain and along the border of Emirate of Ras Al Khaimah, the independent water project (IWP) is one of the largest pure play reverse osmosis plants in the listing.

    Expected to be commercially operational in the third quarter of 2022, UAQ is being delivered as a build, own, operate and transfer (BOOT) with a water purchase agreement (WPA) term of 35 years, with the Federal Electricity and Water Authority (FEWA) as the off-taker.

    The US$797 million project is being constructed by Chinese partner, Gezhouba Group International Engineering, with SIDEM-Veolia delivering the US$255 million engineering and procurement contract. ACWA Power will have a 40 per cent share, with Techton Engineering and Construction 35 per cent.

06. ### DEWA Station M, Dubai - 636,000 m3/day


    ![DEWA Station M Dubai](https://edge.sitecorecloud.io/raiamsterda13f7-raidigitalpdb6c-productionf3f5-ef30/media/project/rai-amsterdam-xmc/aquatech/aquatech/news/2019/4/6-dewa-station-m-dubai.jpg)

    The Dubai Electricity and Water Authority (DEWA) M-Station project is one of the largest power and desalination plants in UAE, producing 636,600 m3/day of potable water and with a total power capacity of 2,885MW.

    The project has been implemented in phases since mid-2010. Completed in 2012, the desalination plant was built in eight units by Italian company, [Fisia](https://www.aquatechtrade.com/water-stories/wastewater/fisia-italimpianti-expands-wastewater-plant) Italimpianti. Multi-Stage Flash (MSF) distillation technology is used, each with a capacity of 80,000 m3/day.

    The combined total cost of the construction and expansion of M-Station, equipped with the latest smart devices and sophisticated heavy-duty technological systems, is AED11.5 billion (US$3.1bn).

    Furthermore, two dual-fuel-fired auxiliary boilers of 390 tons per hour, and 16 fuel-oil storage tanks, each with a capacity of 20,000 cubic meters, with total fuel-oil storage of 320,000 cubic metres.

07. ### Sorek, Israel – 624,000 m3/day


    ![Sorek desalination plants](https://edge.sitecorecloud.io/raiamsterda13f7-raidigitalpdb6c-productionf3f5-ef30/media/project/rai-amsterdam-xmc/aquatech/aquatech/news/2019/5/101/aqd_sorek_720x400.png)

    If Ras Al Khair is considered the heavyweight hybrid of the world then Sorek should be considered the heavyweight [membrane plant](https://www.aquatechtrade.com/water-stories/water-treatment/membranes-are-still-attracting-serious-investment) of the world in operation with an enormous 624,000 m3/day capacity.

    Located 15km south of Tel Aviv in Israel and developed by IDE Technologies, the project was and continues to be unique in the use of 16 inch seawater reverse osmosis membranes but in a vertical formation.

    A further development – Sorek 2 – has since been announced with a capacity of 548,000 m3/day. A total of **seven local and international companies were recently shortlisted to develop the project.**

    Once complete, Sorek 2 will be the sixth desalination plant to operate in Israel alongside Hadera, Ashkelon, the first Sorek, Palmachim and Ashdod.

08. ### Jubail 3A IWP, Saudi Arabia - 600,000 m3/day


    ![Jubail 3A IWP](https://edge.sitecorecloud.io/raiamsterda13f7-raidigitalpdb6c-productionf3f5-ef30/media/project/rai-amsterdam-xmc/aquatech/aquatech/news/2019/4/8-jubail-3a-iwp.jpg)

    With an investment value of US$650 million, the Jubail 3A Independent Water Plant (IWP) will generate 600,000 m3/day of potable water.

    The greenfield seawater reverse osmosis desalination project is set for commercial operation in the fourth quarter of 2022.

    The 25-year water purchase agreement was signed with the Saudi Water Partnership Company (SWPC) by a consortium led by ACWA Power 40.2 per cent, including Gulf Investment Corporation (GIC) and Al Bawani Water & Power Company (AWP).

    Under the terms of the partnership, the consortium led by ACWA Power will design, construct, commission, operate and maintain the desalination plant as well as associated potable water storage and electrical special facilities.

    The Engineering Procurement Construction contract has been awarded to a consortium consisting of Power China, SEPCO-III and Abengoa.

09. ### Sorek 2, Israel – 570,000 m3/day


    ![Sorek 2 Israel](https://edge.sitecorecloud.io/raiamsterda13f7-raidigitalpdb6c-productionf3f5-ef30/media/project/rai-amsterdam-xmc/aquatech/aquatech/news/2019/4/9-sorek-2-israel.jpg)

    In May 2020, IDE Technologies and Bank Leumi were selected as winners of the public-private-partnership (PPP), 25-year tender for the construction of a second, new desalination plant in Sorek.

    IDE's proposal was at an “unprecedented low price” of about NIS1.45 m3 (~ USD 0.41/m3). This reduction in price is predicted to save the water economy about NIS3.3 billion (US$1.02bn) over the period of the facility's operations.

    Similar to Sorek 1, the second plant will also use RO membranes to produce 580,000 m3/day.

    The project will be funded by a consortium of entities, including Bank Leumi and the German KFW Bank, as well as the European Investment Bank (EIB), which will fund a €150 million loan for the project.

    IDE is owned by Alpha Water Partners, controlled by Avshalom Felber and Amir Lang together with Clal Insurance, Ayalon Insurance, Meitav Dash, and other institutional investors.

10. ### Fujairah 2, United Arab Emirates – 591,000 m3/day


    ![Fujairah desalination plants](https://edge.sitecorecloud.io/raiamsterda13f7-raidigitalpdb6c-productionf3f5-ef30/media/project/rai-amsterdam-xmc/aquatech/aquatech/news/2019/5/100/aqd_fujairah_720x400.png)

    A second hybrid to join the list but this time in the UAE, Fujairah 2 stacks up at 591,000 m3/day. This includes multiple components: a 450,000 m3/day thermal plant, a 136,500 m3/day reverse osmosis facility and a 2000 MW power plant.

    The contract was awarded to a consortium made up of Alstom for the power and Sidem (Veolia) for the water, under the name of the Fujairah II Independent Water & Power Production project. Abu Dhabi Water & Electricity (ADWEA) is the off-taker.

    Sidem says the hybrid MED-RO solution helps to match seasonal water and power demands. Furthermore, to protect the plant from algae blooms which can be problematic in the area, a dissolved air flotation (DAF) system is used as a pre-treatment.

    More information on the Fujairah 2 project can be found in the video below:


## Can the big get bigger?

Answering the question of whether desalination capacity will continue to be pushed in the world’s largest plants, Leon Awerbuch, past president of the International Desalination Association (IDA), said: “I believe mega scale desalination plants in the future will get bigger than Ras Al Khair. The Japanese Mega-Ton projects are already under consideration and a 1,500,000 m3/day project in Saudi Arabia is already in planning stage.”

He added: “In the future there will be projects of this scale in Saudi Arabia, China, India and even in Abu Dhabi there is competitive for the scale of 600,000 m3/day. All of these developments will on an Independent Water Project (IWP) or Independent Water and Power Project (IWPP) basis but will be required and guaranteed by governments.

![Scaling Water Reuse: Unlocking Circular Water Economies](https://idrawater.org/wp-content/uploads/2026/06/Scaling-Water-Reuse_-Unlocking-Circular-Water-Economies.jpg)

![](https://idrawater.org/wp-content/uploads/2025/11/Calendar.png)

June 17, 2026

### Scaling Water Reuse: Unlocking Circular Water Economies

![](https://idrawater.org/wp-content/uploads/2025/11/Location.png)
Room 2, Level 4

Water reuse is one of the most scalable solutions to growing water stress, yet policy, financing, regulatory, and public acceptance barriers limit adoption. Global leaders will discuss how reuse can become a mainstream pillar of water security and climate resilience.

![IDRA World Congress 2026](https://idrawater.org/wp-content/uploads/2025/05/Screenshot-2025-11-18-at-9.46.12-PM.png)

![](https://idrawater.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/10/Calendar.png)

November 1-5, 2026

### IDRA World Congress 2026

![](https://idrawater.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/10/Location.png)
Riyadh, Kingdom of Saudi Arabia

Under the Patronage of the Ministry of Environment, Water and Agriculture, and hosted by the Saudi Water Authority, **[the IDRA World Congress in 2026](https://idraworldcongress.org/):** _Driving Global Water Sustainability Through Innovation_ will convene experts, decision-makers, and innovators to advance global desalination and reuse adoption.

[![](https://idrawater.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/10/linkedin-1.png)](https://www.linkedin.com/showcase/idra-world-congress/)

A special word from Fmr. Secretary John F. Kerry, U.S.A. Special Presidential Envoy for Climate Change

#### “Most people will first experience the impact of climate change through water. Two billion people already lack access to safely managed drinking water, and this problem is only going to get worse due to the climate crisis. The world is changing.”

– Fmr. Secretary John F. Kerry, United States Special Presidential Envoy for Climate Change

## Honoring 50 Years of Innovation in Desalination and Water Reuse

We believe in impactful growth. Desalination and reuse technologies have been changing drastically over the last fifty-few years, and we want to showcase the instrumental role of IDRA and its members in driving this development. Explore the history of change here.

## Impact in Numbers Created   Through Desalination and   Water Reuse

## Climate change is intensifying droughts. Water reuse offers a sustainable solution to meet rising water demands and replenish resources. Around 22,000 desalination plants globally provide clean water to over 300 million people.

web.markdown:
# Carlsbad Desalination Plant (USA)

The largest seawater desalination plant in the United States

Carlsbad Project - YouTube

Tap to unmute

[Carlsbad Project](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=hqFIVMqteu8) [IDE Technologies](https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCpceHD-EkmpK1q2R6qcShKQ)

![thumbnail-image](https://yt3.ggpht.com/ytc/AIdro_lnFsTpg2U_g6mRjhWDyO2jhlWSbo2ddDY9Y17Qry6f3A=s68-c-k-c0x00ffffff-no-rj)

IDE Technologies2.29K subscribers

[Watch on](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=hqFIVMqteu8)

![](https://ide-tech.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/10/project_carlsbad.jpg)

![](https://ide-tech.com/wp-content/themes/ide/images/circles-1-52.svg)

## About

An award-winning desalination plant in Carlsbad for the San Diego County Water Authority (SDCWA), that provides potable water to 400,000 consumers

IDE and its partners worked for more than 10 years to develop a desalination solution that meets all the challenging criteria

- **Capacity:** 204,412 m³/day (54MGD)
- **Water Source:** Lagoon Seawater
- **Technology:** Reverse Osmosis (RO)
- **Contract Type:** Engineering ,Supply, Supervision of erection, startup and commisioning, Operation & Maintenance (O&M)
- **Location:** Carlsbad, CA, USA
- **Customer:** Poseidon
- **Offtaker:** San Diego County Water Authority
- **Usage:** Potable Water
- **Commissioning date:** 2015

## Highlights

- #### Cutting edge design



Innovative pretreatment phase, resulting in higher efficiency, reduced energy consumption and high quality water.

- #### Revolution for US desalination



Ground-breaking project financing that relies on a true partnership model.

- #### Environmental breakthrough



First major California infrastructure project to eliminate its carbon footprint.


![](https://ide-tech.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/11/premeatepol1.png)

![](https://ide-tech.com/wp-content/themes/ide/images/project_detail_circle.png)

## Awards

- ![](https://ide-tech.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/10/award_icon.png)



Desalination Deal of the Year 2013, Global Water Intelligence (GWI).

- ![](https://ide-tech.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/10/award_icon.png)



North American Water Deal of the Year, Project Finance Magazine

- ![](https://ide-tech.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/10/award_icon.png)



Far West Deal of the Year 2013, The Bond Buyer financial newspaper.

web.markdown:
![Thumbnail (1920x1080)](https://i.ytimg.com/vi/_b_pRarpLik/maxresdefault.jpg)
# [15 LARGEST Desalination Plants](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_b_pRarpLik)

**Visibility**: Public
**Uploaded by**: [Top Fives](https://www.youtube.com/@topfives)
**Uploaded at**: 2025-02-01
**Published at**: 
**Length**: 29:30
**Views**: 18018
**Likes**: 419
**Category**: Entertainment

## Description

```
As populations increase, and communities develop in regions that would previously have been impossible to live in, the need for freshwater is continuously increasing. While there’s plenty on the planet for everyone, it’s not within reach for many, so some countries have had to look at using technology to solve their shortages. One option is to remove the salt from seawater… but to do this at a volume that makes a difference requires the construction of huge facilities. It’s time, then, to take a tour around the most impressive ones of all, as we visit the 15 largest desalination plants. 

#large #largest #desalination #plant #plants #factory #factories #industry #industrial #water 

Several segments are licensed under creative commons
https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0/legalcode

For more video information, please visit our website.

The Top Fives show brings you informational and entertaining top five videos! Join us and subscribe for more.

Follow Josh on Instagram! Founder and producer of the Top Fives show. Stay up to date with the channel and everything YouTube and business related! https://instagram.com/joshuajosephbaker

Follow us on Facebook!
https://facebook.com/topfivesyoutube

Contact us via the email form here: https://www.youtube.com/topfives/about

Note: The videos featured on the Top Fives channel are for educational and informational purposes. If you have a good idea for a video, leave us a comment! We try to read each and every comment made.
```

## Transcript

desalination plants they transform
seawater into fresh drinkable water and
sometimes these facilities are so big
they process billions of gallons of
water every day so I'm going to count
down 15 of the largest desalination
plants starting with number 15 the mour
plant in India the mour seawater
desalination plant which is near chenai
in the state of Tamil Nadu in India is
one of the largest desalination plants
in South Asia it's located around 19 Mi
or 30 km to the north of Chennai and
this plant plays an important role
dealing with the Region's water scarcity
with a design capacity to produce 265
million gallons or about 100 million
lers of portable water per day it was
commissioned in July of 2010 this mener
plant was built as a public private
partnership between the chenai
Metropolitan water supply and the sewage
board and it was built at a cost of
around $70 million and it was the first
major desalination facility in the
country now this plant uses reverse
osmosis technology that's one of the
most efficient and widely used methods
for desalination this process involves
drawing sea water from the Bay of Bengal
pre-treating it to remove any large
particles and impurities and then
passing it through a high-pressure
membrane now these membranes filter out
the dissolved salts and other
contaminants producing clean drinkable
water within the plant's designs were
energy recovery devices to improve its
efficiency desalination is an energy
intensive process and the use of these
helps reduce redu the overall energy
consumption by reusing energy from the
high-pressure brine steam the mener
plant is hugely important to chenai as
the city is known as the Detroit of
India because of its automobile industry
has experienced growth it provides a
trusted alternative water source
particularly during dry spells when
fresh water sources such as reservoirs
and rivers fall short of meeting demand
it also proved to be surprisingly
reliable even managing to withstand a
devastating Cyclone VA in 2016 and it
was able to resume operation shortly
after the
storm number 14 the adelade desalination
plant in
